questions.q: In a household circuit, a fuse or circuit breaker is designed to:
options: Increase the current flowing through appliances
Prevent too much current from causing a fire
questions.explanation: Fuses and circuit breakers protect circuits by breaking the connection if current becomes dangerously high, preventing overheating and potential fires.
questions.hint: What safety feature stops current if something goes wrong?

questions.q: In humans, eye colour is controlled by multiple genes. Why does this produce a range of eye colours rather than just two distinct types?
options: Because eye colour is only determined by the environment
Because multiple genes contribute additive effects, creating continuous variation
Because eye colour cannot be inherited
Because mutations always occur in eye colour genes
questions.explanation: When multiple genes control a trait, their combined effects produce a spectrum of phenotypes rather than distinct categories. This is polygenic inheritance.
questions.hint: Think about how several genes working together can add up to create a range.

questions.q: A sperm cell is specialised for fertilisation. Which two features help it reach and fertilise an egg?
options: Flagellum for movement and acrosome with enzymes to penetrate egg
questions.explanation: The flagellum (tail) provides movement, while the acrosome releases enzymes that help the sperm penetrate the egg cell membrane.
questions.hint: Think about what the sperm cell needs to do: move quickly and break through the egg.

questions.q: When sodium and chlorine react to form sodium chloride, electrons are transferred from sodium to chlorine. What type of bonding occurs in the product?
questions.explanation: When electrons are transferred from one atom to another, ions are formed. The electrostatic attraction between these oppositely charged ions is called ionic bonding.
questions.hint: What happens when electrons move from one atom to another?

questions.q: A substance has a melting point range of 45°C to 52°C. What does this suggest?
options: It is a gas at room temperature
questions.explanation: A wide melting point range indicates the substance is impure. Pure substances have a sharp, fixed melting point.
questions.hint: Compare to a pure substance which melts at one exact temperature.

questions.q: In a sugar solution, what is the solvent?
questions.explanation: Water is the liquid that dissolves the sugar — the solvent.
questions.hint: The solvent is the liquid part of a solution.

questions.q: Why do decomposers occupy a unique position in food webs rather than being part of a linear food chain?
options: They break down dead organic matter from all trophic levels and return nutrients to the soil, feeding on material from multiple levels
They are not important to ecosystems
questions.explanation: Decomposers consume dead organisms and waste from all trophic levels, not just one level above them, so they interact with the entire food web structure.
questions.hint: Where does a decomposer get its food from compared to a consumer?

questions.q: Which structure in sperm helps it to swim towards the egg?
questions.explanation: The tail (flagellum) of a sperm cell moves side-to-side to propel the sperm forward through the female reproductive tract towards the egg.
questions.hint: It's the long structure at the back of the sperm.

questions.q: Which method separates different coloured dyes in ink?
questions.explanation: Chromatography separates substances by how they move at different rates up absorbent paper.
questions.hint: This method creates a colourful pattern on paper.

questions.q: Liquid → gas is called…
questions.explanation: Liquid to gas is evaporating, like water turning into steam.
questions.hint: What happens to puddles on a sunny day?

questions.q: Which of these is a characteristic that shows continuous variation?
options: Presence or absence of a widow's peak
questions.explanation: Height shows continuous variation - it can be any value across a range. Freckles, earlobes, and widow's peaks show discontinuous variation - you either have them or don't.
questions.hint: Which one can be measured as many different values, not just yes or no?

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of discrete data?
options: The height of a student in cm
The number of leaves on a plant
The mass of a rock in grams
questions.explanation: Discrete data can only have whole number values - you cannot have 3.7 leaves. The other options are continuous (can have decimal values).
questions.hint: Think about whether you could have a decimal/fraction of the measurement.

questions.q: A loudspeaker has a cone that vibrates back and forth very quickly. What does this vibration create?
questions.explanation: When the loudspeaker cone vibrates, it pushes on the air particles around it, creating sound waves that travel outward.
questions.hint: Sound is made by vibration, and the speaker cone is vibrating.

questions.q: What is a hypothesis in science?
options: A prediction based on scientific knowledge
questions.explanation: A hypothesis is an educated guess or prediction that can be tested by an experiment. It is not yet proven.
questions.hint: It's what you think will happen BEFORE you do the experiment.

questions.q: A digital balance reads 45.67 g for the same object each time it is weighed. This measurement is:
questions.explanation: The readings are consistent (precise—to 2 decimal places) and, assuming 45.67 g is the true value, they are also close to the correct value (accurate).
questions.hint: Are the readings close together and close to the true value?

questions.q: Which of the following is the correct order of organisation in living organisms, from smallest to largest?
questions.explanation: The correct hierarchy is: cells combine to form tissues, tissues form organs, organs form systems, and systems make up the organism.
questions.hint: Start with the smallest living unit.

questions.q: What does the cytoplasm do?
options: Contains cell fluid where reactions occur
questions.explanation: The cytoplasm is a gel-like substance that fills the cell and contains dissolved nutrients where many chemical reactions take place.
questions.hint: It's the 'jelly' inside the cell where things happen.

questions.q: Which of the following is a mixture?
questions.explanation: Salt water contains two substances (salt and water) mixed together, making it a mixture.
questions.hint: A mixture has more than one substance that can be separated.

questions.q: Which statement best explains why a parasitic relationship (like fleas on a dog) is different from a predator-prey relationship?
options: Parasites feed on their host over a long period without killing it quickly
Parasites always live outside the body
questions.explanation: Parasites depend on their host's survival for their own survival, so they do not kill the host quickly. Predators kill and eat their prey completely.
questions.hint: Does a flea kill a dog quickly or slowly?

mocks.title: Paper 1 — Mock 11
mocks.instructions: 60 multiple-choice questions. Fresh question set (different from Mocks 1-8). 45 minutes. 1 mark each.

questions.q: Pure water boils at exactly 100°C. A sample of water boils between 99°C and 101°C. What conclusion can you draw?
options: The water is being heated too quickly
questions.explanation: A boiling point range indicates the presence of dissolved impurities. Pure water has a fixed boiling point of exactly 100°C.
questions.hint: Compare the boiling point range to what happens with pure water.

questions.q: Which statement about renewable and non-renewable energy sources is correct?
options: Renewable sources can run out eventually
Solar energy is renewed naturally by the Sun
questions.explanation: Solar energy is renewable because the Sun continuously produces energy and will do so for billions of years. The other statements are false.
questions.hint: 'Renewable' means it is naturally replaced or will last very long.

questions.q: What does the periodic table show?
options: All the compounds in the world
questions.explanation: The periodic table is an organized display of all the known elements.
questions.hint: It's a table that chemists use.

questions.q: What do we call the number of waves that pass a point in one second?
questions.explanation: Frequency measures how many complete waves pass a point per second, measured in Hertz (Hz).
questions.hint: Think about how often waves repeat.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of a qualitative observation?
options: The mass decreased from 10 g to 8 g
The volume of gas was 250 mL
questions.explanation: A qualitative observation describes a quality or characteristic (colour, smell, texture) without numbers. The other options are quantitative—they use measurements.
questions.hint: Qualitative = descriptions without numbers. Which doesn't have a number in it?

questions.q: Which statement about paper chromatography is correct?
options: The solvent moves down the paper by gravity
Dyes with smaller molecules travel further up the paper
The baseline must be drawn in pencil, not pen
All dyes in a mixture travel at the same speed
questions.explanation: The baseline must be in pencil because pen ink would dissolve in the solvent and interfere with the results.
questions.hint: What do you draw the baseline with? Why can't you use certain materials?

questions.q: Which of the following best describes gas particles?
options: Tightly packed and fixed in position
questions.explanation: Gas particles are far apart and move randomly in all directions at high speeds.
questions.hint: Think about air or smoke spreading throughout a room.

questions.q: During freezing, what happens to the particles as a liquid cools down?
options: Particles vibrate faster and move closer together
Particles vibrate slower and arrange into a fixed pattern
questions.explanation: As temperature drops during freezing, particles vibrate less. They slow down enough that forces between them hold them in fixed, orderly positions forming a solid.
questions.hint: What happens to particle vibration as temperature decreases?

questions.q: A student builds a parallel circuit with two identical resistors. How does the total resistance compare to a series circuit with the same two resistors?
options: Both circuits have the same resistance
It depends on the battery voltage
questions.explanation: In parallel circuits, current has multiple paths, so total resistance is lower than in series. For identical resistors, parallel resistance is half the series resistance.
questions.hint: More paths for current = easier flow = lower resistance.

questions.q: Which of the following is the correct order of organisation from smallest to largest?
questions.explanation: Cells are the smallest living units. Similar cells form tissues, tissues form organs, and organs work together in organ systems.
questions.hint: Start with the smallest unit of life and build up.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes the amplitude of a wave?
options: The number of waves per second
The distance the wave travels in one second
The maximum distance a particle moves from its resting position
The distance from one crest to the next trough
questions.explanation: Amplitude is how far a particle moves away from its resting position, measured from the centre to the crest or trough.
questions.hint: Think about how far up and down a particle moves from the middle.

questions.q: Two species of bird live in the same woodland and eat the same insects. Over time, one species begins to hunt only at night while the other hunts during the day. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: The two species are reducing competition by occupying different niches (time periods). Each species now feeds in a different way, allowing both to survive.
questions.hint: What has changed to reduce competition between them?

questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between cells, tissues, and organs in a multicellular organism?
options: Organs are made of cells, tissues are made of organs
Cells are made of tissues, tissues are made of organs
Tissues are made of cells, organs are made of tissues
Organs are made of tissues, tissues are made of organs
questions.explanation: The correct hierarchy is: cells form tissues, tissues combine to form organs. For example, muscle cells form muscle tissue, which is part of the heart organ.
questions.hint: Think of the smallest unit (cells) and how they build up into larger structures.

questions.q: Which statement correctly explains why sodium and chlorine react to form sodium chloride?
options: Sodium and chlorine atoms are identical in size
Sodium atoms transfer electrons to chlorine atoms, forming an ionic compound
questions.explanation: Sodium is a reactive metal that loses electrons to chlorine, a reactive non-metal, forming ionic bonds and the compound NaCl.
questions.hint: Think about what reactive metals and non-metals do during bonding.

questions.q: During sexual reproduction, what is the main advantage of inheriting genetic material from both parents?
options: It creates genetic variation in the population
The offspring is guaranteed to be healthy
It requires less energy than asexual reproduction
questions.explanation: Genetic material from two parents combines in different ways, creating variation. This variation helps populations adapt to changing environments.
questions.hint: How does having genes from two different sources create something new?

questions.q: What is the name of a single complete back-and-forth movement of a vibrating object?
questions.explanation: An oscillation is one complete vibration, going back and forth once.
questions.hint: Think of a pendulum swinging once from one side to the other and back.

questions.q: Which organelle contains genetic information in plant and animal cells?
questions.explanation: The nucleus contains DNA, which carries all genetic information needed to control the cell.
questions.hint: Think of it as the cell's instruction manual.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of discrete data?
options: The height of a student in cm
The number of leaves on a plant
The mass of a rock in grams
questions.explanation: Discrete data can only have whole number values - you cannot have 3.7 leaves. The other options are continuous (can have decimal values).
questions.hint: Think about whether you could have a decimal/fraction of the measurement.

questions.q: On an oscilloscope display, which feature indicates a louder sound?
questions.explanation: Amplitude determines loudness; greater amplitude = louder sound. Frequency determines pitch, and wavelength determines the spacing of waves.
questions.hint: Loudness is related to how much energy the wave carries, shown by wave height.

questions.q: What is variation?
options: Differences between organisms of the same species
The joining of sperm and egg
questions.explanation: Variation refers to the differences in characteristics between individual organisms of the same species.
questions.hint: Think about why your classmates look different from each other.

questions.q: A parasite differs from a predator because a parasite…
options: Lives on or inside its host and feeds without killing it
questions.explanation: Parasites live on or inside a host organism and feed on it over time without immediately killing it (this would harm their food source). Predators hunt, kill and eat their prey immediately.
questions.hint: A parasite needs its host to stay alive for a long time. Why would a predator kill immediately?

questions.q: A mobile phone battery stores 20,000 joules of energy. If the phone uses 10 joules per second, how long will the battery last?
questions.explanation: Time = Energy ÷ Power. Time = 20,000 J ÷ 10 J/s = 2,000 seconds (about 33 minutes).
questions.hint: Power is joules per second. Divide energy by power.

questions.q: Which statement correctly describes the role of the umbilical cord?
options: It produces hormones to control the baby's growth
It carries blood containing nutrients and oxygen from mother to baby
It protects the baby from infections
It removes only waste products from the baby
questions.explanation: The umbilical cord contains blood vessels that transport oxygen, nutrients, and waste between the mother and developing baby.
questions.hint: Think of it like a pipeline connecting the baby to the mother's blood supply.

questions.q: An oscilloscope display shows two sound waves side by side. Wave X has waves that are closer together than Wave Y. What can you conclude?
options: Wave X is louder than Wave Y
Wave X has a shorter wavelength than Wave Y
Wave X travels faster than Wave Y
Wave Y has a higher frequency than Wave X
questions.explanation: Waves that are closer together have a shorter wavelength. Wavelength depends on how the waves are spaced, not on loudness, speed (in the same medium), or frequency directly.
questions.hint: Waves closer together means the distance between peaks is smaller—that's wavelength.

questions.q: In plant cells, the vacuole is mainly used for:
options: Storing water and maintaining cell shape
questions.explanation: The large central vacuole in plant cells stores water, nutrients, and helps the plant cell maintain its rigid shape and firmness.
questions.hint: It keeps the plant cell firm and stores cell sap.

questions.q: Which method would separate sand from water in a muddy sand-water mixture?
questions.explanation: Filtration separates insoluble solids (sand) from liquids (water) using a filter paper.
questions.hint: Sand does not dissolve in water - it is insoluble.

questions.q: When a gas is compressed into a smaller volume at constant temperature, what happens to the pressure?
options: Pressure decreases because particles have less space
Pressure increases because particles collide with walls more frequently
Pressure stays the same because temperature hasn't changed
questions.explanation: Compressing a gas reduces the volume, so particles hit the walls in a smaller space more frequently, increasing pressure even though temperature and particle speed remain constant.
questions.hint: How does reducing space affect how often particles hit the walls?

questions.q: Which statement about ribosomes is correct?
options: They only exist in plant cells
They make proteins for the cell
questions.explanation: Ribosomes are found in all cells and are responsible for synthesizing (making) proteins based on instructions from the nucleus.
questions.hint: Think about what small structures make proteins in cells.

questions.q: In plants, which structure contains the male gamete?
questions.explanation: Pollen contains the male gamete (sperm cell) in plants.
questions.hint: The powder-like substance that causes hay fever.

questions.q: What is a saturated solution?
options: A solution where no more solute can dissolve
A solution made with hot water
questions.explanation: A saturated solution contains the maximum amount of dissolved solute at that temperature.
questions.hint: Think about when you cannot dissolve any more sugar in your tea.

questions.q: What is the chemical symbol for oxygen?
questions.explanation: The chemical symbol for oxygen is O (a single capital letter). O₂ is a molecule of oxygen gas with two atoms.
questions.hint: Chemical symbols are usually one or two letters.

questions.q: In an experiment testing how temperature affects the speed of a reaction, which is the independent variable?
questions.explanation: Temperature is the variable being deliberately changed by the scientist, making it the independent variable.
questions.hint: Which variable does the scientist change intentionally?

questions.q: What is the atomic number of an element?
options: The total number of protons and neutrons
The number of protons in an atom
The number of neutrons in an atom
The mass of the atom in grams
questions.explanation: The atomic number is the number of protons in an atom of an element. This number identifies which element it is.
questions.hint: The atomic number tells you the identity of the element.

questions.q: Which method separates different coloured dyes in ink?
questions.explanation: Chromatography separates substances by how they move at different rates up absorbent paper.
questions.hint: This method creates a colourful pattern on paper.

questions.q: A drum is struck with a drumstick. Which sequence correctly shows the energy transfers?
options: Light energy → Sound energy → Kinetic energy
Kinetic energy → Sound energy → Thermal energy
Kinetic energy → Sound energy → Kinetic energy of air particles
Thermal energy → Kinetic energy → Sound energy
questions.explanation: The drumstick has kinetic energy. When it strikes the drum, it creates sound energy. This sound energy causes air particles to move (kinetic energy of the particles).
questions.hint: Start with the moving drumstick and follow what happens step by step.

questions.q: What happens to the grass population if all the rabbits in a meadow die from disease?
questions.explanation: Rabbits eat grass (they are herbivores). Without rabbits to graze, more grass survives and grows, so the population increases.
questions.hint: Rabbits eat grass. What happens to food when consumers are removed?

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of a variation caused only by genes?
questions.explanation: Blood type is inherited and cannot be changed by the environment. Skin tone can be affected by sun exposure, muscle size by exercise, and hair length by cutting.
questions.hint: Which trait cannot be changed by what you do or where you live?

questions.q: Which organism breaks down dead material and returns nutrients to the soil?
questions.explanation: Decomposers like bacteria and fungi break down dead organisms and recycle nutrients.
questions.hint: These organisms 'decompose' (break down) dead things.

questions.q: Which state of matter has particles that are tightly packed and cannot move around?
questions.explanation: Solid particles are tightly packed in fixed positions and can only vibrate.
questions.hint: Think about something you can hold in your hand that keeps its shape.

questions.q: A power station generates 1,000,000 joules of energy. If it took 1 minute to generate this energy, what was the power output?
questions.explanation: Power = Energy ÷ Time = 1,000,000 J ÷ 60 s = 16,667 W (approximately).
questions.hint: Power is measured in watts. 1 watt = 1 joule per second.

questions.q: What is the total number of atoms in one molecule of calcium carbonate (CaCO₃)?
questions.explanation: Calcium carbonate contains: 1 calcium atom + 1 carbon atom + 3 oxygen atoms = 5 atoms total.
questions.hint: Count Ca(1) + C(1) + O(3) carefully.

questions.q: Which of the following is a control variable in an experiment?
options: The amount of light you change to measure plant growth
The height of the plant that you measure
The type of soil you keep the same for all plants
The time period over which you collect data
questions.explanation: A control variable is kept the same throughout an experiment to ensure a fair test. The soil type is kept constant while other factors change.
questions.hint: Think 'Control' = keeping things the same to make it fair.

questions.q: Why do decomposers occupy a unique position in food webs rather than being part of a linear food chain?
options: They break down dead organic matter from all trophic levels and return nutrients to the soil, feeding on material from multiple levels
They are not important to ecosystems
questions.explanation: Decomposers consume dead organisms and waste from all trophic levels, not just one level above them, so they interact with the entire food web structure.
questions.hint: Where does a decomposer get its food from compared to a consumer?

questions.q: In which state of matter are the particles closest together?
options: All states have particles equally spaced
questions.explanation: Solid particles are closest together because they are tightly packed in fixed positions.
questions.hint: Which state takes up the least amount of space?

questions.q: A kettle has a power rating of 2000 W. Which statement about energy transfer is correct?
options: It transfers 2000 W of heat energy
It stores 2000 J in the water
questions.explanation: Power is measured in watts (W), where 1 W = 1 J/s. So 2000 W means 2000 joules of energy transferred every second.
questions.hint: Power = energy ÷ time. What is power measured in joules per?

questions.q: What is the name of the variable you measure or observe as a result of your experiment?
questions.explanation: The dependent variable is what you measure - it is the effect or result that depends on what you changed (the independent variable).
questions.hint: It 'depends on' the independent variable. It is the result you measure.

questions.q: Which of the following is a mixture?
questions.explanation: Salt water contains two substances (salt and water) mixed together, making it a mixture.
questions.hint: A mixture has more than one substance that can be separated.

questions.q: Which of these is a function of the cell wall?
questions.explanation: The cell wall is a rigid structure around plant cells that provides structural support and protection.
questions.hint: It's only found in plant cells and helps them stay rigid and strong.

questions.q: Which pair of elements would you expect to have the most similar chemical properties?
questions.explanation: Chlorine and bromine are both in Group 17 (halogens) of the periodic table. They have similar electron arrangements in their outer shells, making their properties similar.
questions.hint: Look at which elements are in the same group (column) of the periodic table.

questions.q: What is the correct order of state changes from solid to gas?
questions.explanation: A solid first melts to become a liquid, then the liquid evaporates to become a gas.
questions.hint: Think about what happens to ice when it warms up.

questions.q: A student measures the temperature of boiling water five times and records: 98°C, 99°C, 100°C, 100°C, 101°C. What is the mean temperature?
questions.hint: Add all five values together, then divide by 5.

questions.q: Which state of matter has a fixed shape and a fixed volume?
questions.explanation: Only solids have both a fixed shape and fixed volume because their particles are in fixed positions.
questions.hint: Which one doesn't change shape when you move it?

questions.q: Why do cells that line the small intestine have many finger-like projections called villi?
options: To increase surface area for absorption of nutrients
To help the intestine move food along
questions.explanation: The villi greatly increase the surface area of the epithelial tissue, allowing more efficient absorption of digested nutrients.
questions.hint: What advantage does a larger surface area give for absorption?

questions.q: Which type of data is 'colour of a flower'?
questions.explanation: Categoric data consists of words or labels, like colours, names, or types. It cannot be measured numerically.
questions.hint: Is this a word/label or a number you can measure?

questions.q: What is the SI unit of electrical current?
questions.explanation: The ampere (A) is the SI unit used to measure electrical current.
questions.hint: Think of the device that measures current — an ammeter.

questions.q: Which separation method uses paper to separate insoluble solids from liquids?
questions.explanation: Filtration uses filter paper with tiny holes. Liquids pass through but solid particles are trapped.
questions.hint: The word contains 'filter'.

questions.q: Which of the following is made in the ovaries?
questions.explanation: Egg cells are produced in the ovaries in females.
questions.hint: The female reproductive organ that produces eggs.

questions.q: A food web shows multiple interconnected food chains in an ecosystem. Why is a food web more useful than a single food chain for understanding real ecosystems?
options: Real organisms eat multiple food sources, and food webs show how removing one species affects many others
Food chains are too easy to draw
Food webs show the same information but faster
questions.explanation: Food webs realistically show that most organisms have multiple food sources and are food sources for multiple predators. This helps predict cascading effects when one species is removed or population changes.
questions.hint: Does a rabbit only eat one type of plant?

questions.q: Which symbol represents a cell (battery) in an electrical circuit diagram?
options: A long line and a short line
questions.explanation: A cell is represented by one long line and one short line. A battery is two or more cells.
questions.hint: The long line is the positive terminal.

questions.q: Which of the following is a primary consumer in a food chain?
questions.explanation: A primary consumer eats plants (producers). The zebra is a herbivore that eats grass.
questions.hint: Primary consumers eat plants, not other animals.

mocks.title: Paper 1 — Mock 12
mocks.instructions: 60 multiple-choice questions. Fresh question set (different from Mocks 1-8). 45 minutes. 1 mark each.

questions.q: Which separation technique would be most suitable for separating a mixture of two immiscible liquids with similar boiling points?
questions.explanation: A separating funnel is used to separate immiscible liquids (liquids that do not mix) regardless of their boiling points.
questions.hint: Immiscible means the liquids don't mix - think of oil and water.

questions.q: Which type of data is the colour of a flower?
questions.explanation: Categoric data is descriptive - it is a label or category (like colours, plant species, or names). It is not a number.
questions.hint: Can you count the colours on a scale, or are they just different types/categories?

questions.q: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Electrical resistance is measured in ohms (Ω).
questions.hint: It sounds like the Greek letter omega.

questions.q: Which of the following best explains why a solid has a fixed shape?
options: Particles in a solid don't move at all
Particles are tightly packed and vibrate in fixed positions around strong attractive forces
Particles are very large and cannot move
The solid is held in place by gravity
questions.explanation: In a solid, particles vibrate around fixed positions due to strong forces between them, giving the solid its rigid shape.
questions.hint: Are particles in a solid completely still or do they have some movement?

questions.q: A sound wave travels through air at 340 m/s. If the wavelength is 0.68 m, what is the frequency of the sound?
questions.explanation: Using the wave equation v = f × λ, we rearrange to f = v ÷ λ = 340 ÷ 0.68 = 500 Hz.
questions.hint: Use the formula: frequency = speed ÷ wavelength

questions.q: Two species of bird live in the same woodland and eat the same insects. Over time, one species begins to hunt only at night while the other hunts during the day. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: The two species are reducing competition by occupying different niches (time periods). Each species now feeds in a different way, allowing both to survive.
questions.hint: What has changed to reduce competition between them?

questions.q: Which of the following is a compound?
questions.explanation: Carbon dioxide (CO₂) is made of two different elements chemically bonded together, making it a compound. The others are elements.
questions.hint: A compound must contain at least two different elements joined together.

questions.q: What is the main function of the cell membrane?
options: To control what enters and leaves the cell
questions.explanation: The cell membrane controls which substances can enter and leave the cell.
questions.hint: Think about what acts as a barrier or gatekeeper.

questions.q: In paper chromatography, a dye travels 6 cm while the solvent travels 10 cm. What is the Rf value?
questions.explanation: Rf = distance travelled by substance ÷ distance travelled by solvent = 6 ÷ 10 = 0.6
questions.hint: Rf value = divide the distances

questions.q: Two identical bulbs are connected in series to a battery. If one bulb fails and breaks the circuit, what happens to the other bulb?
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, if one component breaks, the circuit is broken and current cannot flow anywhere. Both bulbs go off.
questions.hint: In series, there is only one path for current to follow.

questions.q: Which state of matter has a fixed shape and a fixed volume?
questions.explanation: Only solids have both a fixed shape and fixed volume because their particles are in fixed positions.
questions.hint: Which one doesn't change shape when you move it?

questions.q: What do we call the number of waves that pass a point in one second?
questions.explanation: Frequency measures how many complete waves pass a point per second, measured in Hertz (Hz).
questions.hint: Think about how often waves repeat.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of a predator?
questions.explanation: A predator is an animal that hunts and eats other animals. A lion hunts other animals for food.
questions.hint: Think of an animal that hunts other animals for food.

questions.q: Which statement about renewable and non-renewable energy sources is correct?
options: Renewable sources can run out eventually
Solar energy is renewed naturally by the Sun
questions.explanation: Solar energy is renewable because the Sun continuously produces energy and will do so for billions of years. The other statements are false.
questions.hint: 'Renewable' means it is naturally replaced or will last very long.

questions.q: What does evaporation leave behind when salt water is heated?
questions.explanation: Salt does not evaporate with water, so it stays in the container as crystals.
questions.hint: Which part has a higher boiling point?

questions.q: Human skin colour is controlled by multiple genes. A child born to parents with different skin colours often has an intermediate shade. This is best explained by…
options: Polygenic inheritance, where alleles combine additively
Environmental factors alone determining skin colour
The child always inheriting skin colour from one parent only
questions.explanation: Multiple genes each contribute small effects to skin colour, producing a range. Alleles from both parents combine additively, resulting in intermediate phenotypes.
questions.hint: Many genes + additive effects = what type of variation?

questions.q: What is the symbol for voltage in equations?
questions.explanation: V is the symbol for voltage, measured in volts (V).
questions.hint: V stands for 'volt'.

questions.q: Plant cells have a cell wall made of cellulose. What is the main advantage of this structure?
options: It provides rigidity and structural support to the plant
It controls what enters and leaves the cell
questions.explanation: The cell wall is rigid and provides mechanical support, allowing plants to stand upright and maintain their shape.
questions.hint: Why can plants stand up without a skeleton?

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT an element?
questions.explanation: Salt is a compound made of sodium and chlorine atoms bonded together, not an element.
questions.hint: Which one is made from two different elements?

questions.q: Why is the placenta an important organ during pregnancy?
options: It is where the baby's heart develops
It allows nutrient and oxygen exchange between mother and baby
It controls how fast the baby grows
questions.explanation: The placenta is embedded in the uterus wall and allows oxygen and nutrients to pass from mother's blood to baby's blood, and waste products to pass back.
questions.hint: What structure needs to pass materials between two separate blood systems?

questions.q: Which statement correctly explains why sodium and chlorine react to form sodium chloride?
options: Sodium and chlorine atoms are identical in size
Sodium atoms transfer electrons to chlorine atoms, forming an ionic compound
questions.explanation: Sodium is a reactive metal that loses electrons to chlorine, a reactive non-metal, forming ionic bonds and the compound NaCl.
questions.hint: Think about what reactive metals and non-metals do during bonding.

questions.q: Which statement about paper chromatography is correct?
options: The solvent moves down the paper by gravity
Dyes with smaller molecules travel further up the paper
The baseline must be drawn in pencil, not pen
All dyes in a mixture travel at the same speed
questions.explanation: The baseline must be in pencil because pen ink would dissolve in the solvent and interfere with the results.
questions.hint: What do you draw the baseline with? Why can't you use certain materials?

questions.q: A sound wave travels through air at 340 m/s. If the wavelength of a note is 0.68 m, what is the frequency of this note?
questions.explanation: Using the equation: frequency = speed ÷ wavelength. f = 340 ÷ 0.68 = 500 Hz.
questions.hint: Use the wave equation: speed = frequency × wavelength, or rearrange to find frequency.

questions.q: A digital balance reads 45.67 g for the same object each time it is weighed. This measurement is:
questions.explanation: The readings are consistent (precise—to 2 decimal places) and, assuming 45.67 g is the true value, they are also close to the correct value (accurate).
questions.hint: Are the readings close together and close to the true value?

questions.q: Which statement about asexual reproduction is correct?
options: Offspring are genetically identical to the parent
It involves the fusion of gametes
questions.explanation: Asexual reproduction uses only one parent and produces offspring that are clones - genetically identical. Sexual reproduction involves two parents and creates variation.
questions.hint: Does asexual reproduction need sperm and egg to mix?

questions.q: Which of the following is a consumer in a food chain?
questions.explanation: A consumer is an organism that eats other organisms for food. The rabbit eats grass, making it a consumer. Plants are producers, and soil is neither.
questions.hint: A consumer has to eat something else to survive.

questions.q: Which statement best describes competition in an ecosystem?
options: Two species hunting each other for food
Two species fighting over the same resources like food or space
One species living inside another species
Two species helping each other to survive
questions.explanation: Competition occurs when organisms fight over limited resources such as food, water, light, or space. This can happen between species (interspecific) or within a species (intraspecific).
questions.hint: Competition is about fighting over what?

questions.q: Which statement about the periodic table is correct?
options: Elements are arranged by colour from left to right
Elements in the same period have similar chemical properties
Elements in the same group have similar chemical properties
All elements in row 2 are gases
questions.explanation: Elements in the same group (vertical column) have similar chemical properties because they have the same number of outer shell electrons.
questions.hint: Remember: groups are columns, periods are rows.

questions.q: What is the name of the variable you measure or observe as a result of your experiment?
questions.explanation: The dependent variable is what you measure - it is the effect or result that depends on what you changed (the independent variable).
questions.hint: It 'depends on' the independent variable. It is the result you measure.

questions.q: Which tube carries the egg from the ovary to the uterus?
questions.explanation: The oviduct (fallopian tube) carries the egg from the ovary to the uterus.
questions.hint: It is also called the fallopian tube.

questions.q: Which of the following is a primary consumer in a food chain?
questions.explanation: A primary consumer eats plants (producers). The zebra is a herbivore that eats grass.
questions.hint: Primary consumers eat plants, not other animals.

questions.q: Which type of data is 'colour of a flower'?
questions.explanation: Categoric data consists of words or labels, like colours, names, or types. It cannot be measured numerically.
questions.hint: Is this a word/label or a number you can measure?

questions.q: In plants, which structure contains the male gamete?
questions.explanation: Pollen contains the male gamete (sperm cell) in plants.
questions.hint: The powder-like substance that causes hay fever.

questions.q: Liquid → gas is called…
questions.explanation: Liquid to gas is evaporating, like water turning into steam.
questions.hint: What happens to puddles on a sunny day?

questions.q: In plant cells, the vacuole is mainly used for:
options: Storing water and maintaining cell shape
questions.explanation: The large central vacuole in plant cells stores water, nutrients, and helps the plant cell maintain its rigid shape and firmness.
questions.hint: It keeps the plant cell firm and stores cell sap.

questions.q: What is the smallest particle of an element called?
questions.explanation: An atom is the smallest particle of an element that still has the properties of that element.
questions.hint: Think about what the smallest unit is...

questions.q: Which organelle is responsible for releasing energy in both plant and animal cells through aerobic respiration?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria are the powerhouses of the cell, breaking down glucose to release energy in the form of ATP during aerobic respiration.
questions.hint: Think about which organelle produces the energy currency of the cell.

questions.q: What is the SI unit of electrical current?
questions.explanation: The SI unit of electrical current is the Amp (ampere).
questions.hint: An ammeter measures this unit.

questions.q: What is the name of the cell that is produced when a sperm and egg join together?
questions.explanation: A zygote is the cell formed when a sperm nucleus fuses with an egg nucleus during fertilisation.
questions.hint: It's what forms immediately after fertilisation.

questions.q: A nerve cell has a very long extension called an axon. Why is this adaptation useful?
options: To transmit electrical signals over long distances in the body
To absorb oxygen from the air
questions.explanation: The axon allows nerve cells to send signals quickly from the brain or spinal cord to distant parts of the body.
questions.hint: Think about what nerve cells need to do to communicate with distant body parts.

questions.q: What does the wavelength of a wave measure?
options: The time taken for one wave
The distance from one wave crest to the next crest
questions.explanation: Wavelength is the distance between two identical points on a wave, such as from one crest to the next crest.
questions.hint: Look at the distance between the highest points of a wave.

questions.q: In a stable ecosystem, what does the cycling of nutrients between biotic and abiotic components primarily depend on?
options: Decomposers breaking down dead material and returning elements to the soil
questions.explanation: Decomposers are essential for nutrient cycling because they break down dead organisms and waste, returning nutrients (like nitrogen and carbon) to the soil and atmosphere so producers can use them again.
questions.hint: What organism breaks down dead leaves and returns nutrients to soil?

questions.q: A student repeats a mass measurement four times and gets: 25.0 g, 25.1 g, 25.0 g, 25.2 g. The actual mass is 35.0 g. Which statement is true?
options: The results are accurate but not precise
The results are precise but not accurate
The results are both accurate and precise
The results are neither accurate nor precise
questions.explanation: Results are close together (25.0–25.2 g) so they are precise. But they are far from 35.0 g so they are not accurate—there is a systematic error.
questions.hint: Check if readings are close to each other AND close to the true value.

questions.q: Which method separates different coloured dyes in ink?
questions.explanation: Chromatography separates substances by how they move at different rates up absorbent paper.
questions.hint: This method creates a colourful pattern on paper.

questions.q: An element has the electron configuration 2,8,7. Which of the following statements is true?
options: It is an alkali metal with atomic number 17
It is a halogen with atomic number 17
It is a transition metal with atomic number 17
It is a noble gas with atomic number 18
questions.explanation: An element with 2,8,7 electrons has 17 protons (atomic number 17), making it chlorine. With 7 electrons in the outermost shell, it is in Group 17 (halogens), not a noble gas.
questions.hint: Count the total electrons to find the atomic number. What group has 7 outer electrons?

questions.q: Which of the following is the correct order of organisation from smallest to largest?
questions.explanation: Cells are the smallest living units. Similar cells form tissues, tissues form organs, and organs work together in organ systems.
questions.hint: Start with the smallest unit of life and build up.

questions.q: A red blood cell is specialised to carry oxygen. Which feature helps it do this job most effectively?
options: It has a thick cell wall
questions.explanation: Haemoglobin is a protein in red blood cells that binds to oxygen and transports it around the body.
questions.hint: Think about what substance red blood cells need to carry.

questions.q: Liquid → gas is called…
questions.explanation: Liquid to gas is evaporating, like water turning into steam when heated.
questions.hint: What happens to puddles on a sunny day?

questions.q: A drum is struck and produces a sound. Which of the following describes the motion of the drum head?
options: It moves in a straight line away from you
questions.explanation: When struck, the drum head oscillates (vibrates) up and down repeatedly, creating compressions and rarefactions in the air that form sound waves.
questions.hint: Think about what happens to the surface of a drum when you hit it.

questions.q: Which of the following is a conductor?
questions.explanation: Aluminium is a metal that allows electricity to flow through it easily — it is a conductor.
questions.hint: Conductors are usually shiny metals.

questions.q: What term describes when one animal hunts and eats another animal?
questions.explanation: Predation is the relationship where a predator hunts and eats prey.
questions.hint: Think of a word that sounds like 'predator'.

questions.q: In a sugar solution, what is the solvent?
questions.explanation: The solvent is the liquid that dissolves the solute. Water dissolves the sugar.
questions.hint: Which part is the liquid?

questions.q: What is the name of the process when liquid → solid?
questions.explanation: Liquid to solid is freezing, like water turning into ice.
questions.hint: This is the opposite of melting.

questions.q: Why does a puddle of water eventually disappear on a sunny day without reaching 100°C?
options: The water is absorbed into the ground
Evaporation occurs at the surface where some particles have enough energy to escape
Wind blows all the water away
questions.explanation: Evaporation happens at any temperature when particles at the surface have enough kinetic energy to overcome forces and escape into the air.
questions.hint: Not all particles need the same energy to leave the liquid surface.

questions.q: Why is it important to use appropriate units when recording scientific measurements?
options: It makes the work look neater
It ensures others can understand and interpret your results correctly
It reduces the need for repeats
It automatically makes the experiment more accurate
questions.explanation: Units (g, cm, °C, mL, etc.) communicate the type and scale of measurement. Without them, results are meaningless and others cannot compare or use your data.
questions.hint: What would '25' mean without knowing if it's grams, kilograms, or millilitres?

questions.q: Which state of matter has particles that are tightly packed and vibrate in fixed positions?
questions.explanation: Solid particles are tightly packed together and can only vibrate in fixed positions, giving solids a definite shape and volume.
questions.hint: Think of something hard like a rock or metal.

questions.q: Which renewable energy source is most reliable because it works day and night?
questions.explanation: Geothermal power uses heat from inside the Earth, which is constant day and night. Solar only works during daylight and wind varies throughout the day.
questions.hint: Which energy source does not depend on weather or time of day?

questions.q: Which of the following is a transverse wave?
options: A wave on a stretched string
A compression wave in a spring
questions.explanation: A wave on a stretched string oscillates perpendicular (at right angles) to the direction of wave travel, making it transverse. Sound waves are longitudinal (parallel to travel direction).
questions.hint: Transverse waves vibrate perpendicular to the direction the wave travels.

questions.q: Which piece of equipment would be most suitable for measuring exactly 25 mL of liquid in a chemistry experiment?
questions.explanation: A pipette or burette measures liquids very precisely. A measuring cylinder is less precise. Beakers are for rough estimates only.
questions.hint: Which equipment is designed for precise volume measurement?

questions.q: Why is distillation used to separate salt from seawater but not to separate sand from seawater?
options: Sand dissolves in water but salt does not
Salt has a higher boiling point than water
Sand does not dissolve in water, so it can be filtered out first
Distillation only works with dissolved solids
questions.explanation: Sand is insoluble and settles, so it can be removed by filtration before distillation. Distillation then separates the dissolved salt from water.
questions.hint: Think about what each substance does in water - does it dissolve or not?

mocks.title: Paper 2 — Mock 1
mocks.instructions: Short-answer + skills questions. Total 48 marks. 45 minutes.

questions.q: Explain how fractional distillation separates a mixture of ethanol (boiling point 78°C) and water (100°C).
questions.modelAnswer: The mixture is heated. Ethanol has the lower boiling point so it evaporates first; its vapour rises up the fractionating column. Water vapour mostly condenses on the column and runs back, while ethanol vapour passes into the condenser, cools, condenses and is collected first. As temperature rises toward 100°C, water can then be collected separately.
questions.hint: Which evaporates first and why?
questions.examMistake: Saying water comes off first — ethanol's boiling point is lower.

questions.q: Explain why taking repeat readings improves the mean but cannot remove a systematic error, using an example.
questions.modelAnswer: Repeats let you average out random errors that scatter above and below the true value, so the mean is closer to the true value. A systematic error shifts every reading by the same amount in the same direction (e.g. a stopwatch always started 0.5 s late, or a balance with a zero error), so the average is still wrong by that amount. Repeating does not detect or remove it — you must fix the method or equipment.
questions.hint: Random errors cancel out; a constant shift does not.
questions.examMistake: Claiming averaging fixes all errors.

questions.q: State two differences between a palisade leaf cell and a root hair cell, linked to their jobs.
questions.modelAnswer: A palisade cell has many chloroplasts and is near the top of the leaf to absorb light for photosynthesis; a root hair cell has no chloroplasts (it is underground in the dark) but has a long projection for a large surface area to absorb water and minerals.
questions.hint: One captures light, one absorbs water.
questions.examMistake: Giving features without linking them to the job.

questions.q: A student claims 'the current is used up as it goes round the circuit, which is why the bulb lights'. Explain what is wrong and give the correct idea.
questions.modelAnswer: Current is not used up — the same current flows all the way round a series circuit and returns to the cell. What is actually transferred is energy: the cell gives the charges energy (voltage), and the bulb transfers that energy to light and heat as the charges pass through. The charges keep flowing; it is the energy that is transferred.
questions.hint: Distinguish 'current' from 'energy'.
questions.examMistake: The classic misconception that current is consumed by components.

questions.q: Explain the difference between a random error and a systematic error, and how each can be reduced.
questions.modelAnswer: A random error causes small differences each time you measure (e.g. timing with a stopwatch); it is reduced by repeating readings and taking a mean. A systematic error is a fault in the method or equipment that shifts every reading the same way (e.g. using a beaker not a measuring cylinder); repeats do not fix it — you must change the method or equipment.
questions.hint: One is fixed by repeats, the other isn't.
questions.examMistake: Saying 'repeat the experiment' fixes systematic errors — it does not.

questions.q: Define the word 'habitat'.
questions.modelAnswer: A habitat is the place where an organism lives.
questions.hint: Where does the animal live?
questions.examMistake: Confusing habitat (the place) with ecosystem (community + surroundings).

questions.q: Explain the difference between a solution being concentrated and being saturated.
questions.modelAnswer: A concentrated solution has a large amount of solute dissolved in the solvent, but you could still dissolve more. A saturated solution has so much solute that no more will dissolve at that temperature; any extra solute stays as solid at the bottom.
questions.hint: Can you still dissolve more?
questions.examMistake: Using 'concentrated' and 'saturated' as if they mean the same thing.

questions.q: State what 'current' means.
questions.modelAnswer: Current is the flow of electric charge around a circuit, measured in amps.
questions.hint: Think of it as a flow.
questions.examMistake: Confusing current (flow) with voltage (push).

questions.q: A student views onion cells under ×100, then changes to the ×400 objective and the image goes blurry and darker. Explain why and what to do.
questions.modelAnswer: At higher magnification you see a smaller area, less light reaches the eye so the image is darker, and the focus changes so it looks blurry. To fix it, refocus using only the fine focus knob and increase the light using the diaphragm/light source. Do not use the coarse focus on high power as it can crack the slide.
questions.hint: Think about light and which focus knob is safe on high power.
questions.examMistake: Using coarse focus on high power risks breaking the slide.

questions.q: On an oscilloscope, how does a loud sound look different from a quiet sound?
questions.modelAnswer: A loud sound shows a wave with a larger amplitude (taller wave), while a quiet sound shows a wave with a smaller amplitude (shorter wave). The pitch (how close together the waves are) is unchanged by loudness.
questions.hint: Loudness changes the height of the wave.
questions.examMistake: Saying loud sounds look closer together — that's pitch, not loudness.

questions.q: Explain why offspring resemble their parents but are not identical to them (in sexual reproduction).
questions.modelAnswer: Offspring inherit genes from both parents, carried on chromosomes in the nucleus, so they show features from each parent and therefore resemble them. However, because they get a mixture of genes — half from each parent in a unique combination — they are not identical to either parent or to their siblings.
questions.hint: Genes come from two parents in a new combination.
questions.examMistake: Forgetting that genes come from BOTH parents.

questions.q: Give three ways the properties of metals differ from non-metals.
questions.modelAnswer: Metals are usually shiny while non-metals are often dull. Metals are good conductors of heat and electricity, whereas non-metals are usually poor conductors (insulators). Metals are malleable and can be bent or hammered into shape, while solid non-metals are brittle and snap. (Metals also generally have higher melting points and most are solid at room temperature.)
questions.hint: Appearance, conductivity, and what happens when you bend them.
questions.examMistake: Giving only one property or vague answers like 'metals are hard'.

questions.q: Describe what happens to the particles as a substance is cooled from a gas all the way to a solid.
questions.modelAnswer: As a gas cools, the particles lose energy and slow down. They come closer together and the gas condenses into a liquid, where particles touch but can still slide. On further cooling they lose more energy, move less, and arrange into a fixed regular pattern as the liquid freezes into a solid, where they only vibrate on the spot.
questions.hint: Follow energy decreasing: gas → liquid → solid.
questions.examMistake: Skipping the liquid stage or forgetting energy is being lost.

questions.q: Explain why energy is lost between levels of a food chain and what this means for the number of organisms at each level.
questions.modelAnswer: At each level, organisms use most of the energy they take in for life processes such as movement, respiration and keeping warm, and energy is lost in waste (faeces) and as heat. Only about 10% is stored in the body and passed on when eaten. Because so little energy reaches the next level, there is less energy to support organisms higher up, so there are fewer organisms (and less total mass) at each higher level — forming a pyramid shape.
questions.hint: Where does the energy go, and how does that shape the numbers?
questions.examMistake: Saying energy 'disappears' rather than being used/lost as heat and waste.

mocks.title: Paper 2 — Mock 2
mocks.instructions: Short-answer + skills questions. Total 46 marks. 45 minutes.

questions.q: A cell has a cell wall, a nucleus, a vacuole and chloroplasts. State whether it is a plant or animal cell and justify using TWO features.
questions.modelAnswer: It is a plant cell. It has a cellulose cell wall, which animal cells do not have, and it has chloroplasts for photosynthesis, which are only found in plant cells. (The large permanent vacuole also supports this.)
questions.hint: Which features are plant-only?
questions.examMistake: Naming features that are in both cells (like nucleus) as evidence.

questions.q: Explain how muscles work in antagonistic pairs at the elbow.
questions.modelAnswer: Muscles can only pull, not push, so they work in antagonistic pairs. To bend the arm, the biceps contracts and the triceps relaxes. To straighten the arm, the triceps contracts and the biceps relaxes.
questions.hint: One pulls while the other relaxes.
questions.examMistake: Saying a muscle 'pushes' — muscles only pull.

questions.q: Compare the adaptations of an egg cell and a sperm cell to their functions.
questions.modelAnswer: A sperm cell is small with a tail (flagellum) so it can swim to the egg, and it has many mitochondria to provide energy for swimming; it carries the father's genetic information in its nucleus. An egg cell is much larger and cannot move; it contains a food store (yolk) to nourish the early embryo and the mother's genetic information, and after fertilisation its membrane changes to stop other sperm entering. Both carry a nucleus with genes, but each is specialised for its role.
questions.hint: Sperm = small and mobile; egg = large with food store.
questions.examMistake: Listing features without linking them to fertilisation/nourishing the embryo.

questions.q: Name the piece of equipment you should use to measure exactly 25 cm³ of water, and how you read it.
questions.modelAnswer: A measuring cylinder. Read the volume at the bottom of the meniscus with your eye level with the liquid.
questions.hint: Not a beaker — and where do you look?
questions.examMistake: A beaker is not accurate enough; that would be a systematic error.

questions.q: A 3 V cell lights two identical bulbs. Compare the brightness and the voltage across each bulb when they are connected (a) in series and (b) in parallel.
questions.modelAnswer: In series, the 3 V is shared, so each bulb has about 1.5 V across it and both are dim. In parallel, each branch gets the full 3 V across each bulb, so each bulb is bright (as bright as a single bulb). Parallel bulbs are brighter because they each receive the full voltage.
questions.hint: How is the 3 V divided in each arrangement?
questions.examMistake: Saying parallel bulbs share the voltage — they each get the full voltage.

questions.q: Predator and prey populations often rise and fall in repeating cycles. Explain why, using foxes and rabbits.
questions.modelAnswer: When rabbits are plentiful, foxes have lots of food so the fox population grows. More foxes eat more rabbits, so the rabbit population then falls. With fewer rabbits, many foxes starve and the fox population falls. With fewer foxes, rabbits can breed and recover, so rabbit numbers rise again — and the cycle repeats. The predator peak follows the prey peak.
questions.hint: Follow the chain of cause and effect round in a loop.
questions.examMistake: Not explaining the time delay — predator numbers follow prey numbers.

questions.q: Plan, in outline, a fair-test investigation into how the length of time a gummy bear is soaked in water affects its length. Include the variables and how you would make it reliable.
questions.modelAnswer: Independent variable: time soaked (e.g. 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 minutes). Dependent variable: length of the gummy bear in mm. Control variables: same starting size/type of gummy bear, same volume and temperature of water, same way of measuring length. Method: measure starting length, soak for the set time, remove, dry gently and measure again. Repeat each time at least three times and take a mean, ignoring anomalies, then plot length against time as a line graph and describe the trend.
questions.hint: This is the gummy-bear practical — name variables, repeats, and the graph.
questions.examMistake: Forgetting to control water volume/temperature, or not repeating for a mean.

questions.q: Explain why sound travels faster in water than in air.
questions.modelAnswer: In water the particles are much closer together than in air. Because the particles are closer, the vibrations are passed from one particle to the next more quickly, so the sound wave travels faster in water than in the more spread-out air.
questions.hint: Compare how close the particles are.
questions.examMistake: Saying water is 'thicker' without explaining particle spacing.

questions.q: Explain how fractional distillation separates a mixture of ethanol (boiling point 78°C) and water (100°C).
questions.modelAnswer: The mixture is heated. Ethanol has the lower boiling point so it evaporates first; its vapour rises up the fractionating column. Water vapour mostly condenses on the column and runs back, while ethanol vapour passes into the condenser, cools, condenses and is collected first. As temperature rises toward 100°C, water can then be collected separately.
questions.hint: Which evaporates first and why?
questions.examMistake: Saying water comes off first — ethanol's boiling point is lower.

questions.q: Classify each as element, compound or mixture and justify: (a) oxygen gas O₂, (b) carbon dioxide CO₂, (c) air.
questions.modelAnswer: (a) Oxygen O₂ is an element because it contains only one type of atom (oxygen), even though two are joined. (b) Carbon dioxide CO₂ is a compound because it is two different elements (carbon and oxygen) chemically bonded in a fixed ratio. (c) Air is a mixture because it contains several different substances (nitrogen, oxygen, carbon dioxide, etc.) that are not chemically joined and could be separated physically.
questions.hint: One atom type = element; bonded different atoms = compound; unbonded = mixture.
questions.examMistake: Calling O₂ a compound because it has two atoms — they are the same element.

questions.q: Explain why you can smell hot food from further away than cold food.
questions.modelAnswer: Smell is caused by particles from the food diffusing through the air to your nose. When the food is hot, its particles (and the air particles) have more energy and move faster, so they diffuse and spread out more quickly and reach further before becoming too dilute to detect — so you smell hot food from a greater distance.
questions.hint: Temperature affects how fast particles diffuse.
questions.examMistake: Not connecting temperature to particle speed and diffusion rate.

mocks.title: Paper 2 — Mock 3
mocks.instructions: Short-answer + skills questions. Total 46 marks. 45 minutes.

questions.q: A cell has a cell wall, a nucleus, a vacuole and chloroplasts. State whether it is a plant or animal cell and justify using TWO features.

questions.hint: Which features are plant-only?
questions.examMistake: Naming features that are in both cells (like nucleus) as evidence.

questions.q: Sea water can be made drinkable by distillation. Explain why this works but is expensive.
questions.modelAnswer: Distillation works because heating the sea water evaporates pure water vapour, leaving the dissolved salts behind; the vapour is condensed back to pure liquid water. It is expensive because a lot of energy (heat) is needed to boil large volumes of water, and energy/fuel costs money.
questions.hint: What stays behind, and why is the energy a problem?
questions.examMistake: Not explaining why energy makes it costly.

questions.q: Name the piece of equipment you should use to measure exactly 25 cm³ of water, and how you read it.
questions.modelAnswer: A measuring cylinder. Read the volume at the bottom of the meniscus with your eye level with the liquid.
questions.hint: Not a beaker — and where do you look?
questions.examMistake: A beaker is not accurate enough; that would be a systematic error.

questions.q: Describe how you could decide whether a clear, colourless liquid is an element, a compound or a mixture.
questions.modelAnswer: First check if it can be separated by a physical method such as distillation or evaporation: if something is left behind or different liquids come off at different temperatures, it is a mixture. If it stays pure (boils at one fixed temperature) it is a pure substance. To tell if that pure substance is an element or compound, try to break it down chemically (e.g. by electrolysis): a compound can be split into simpler substances, but an element cannot be broken down into anything simpler.
questions.hint: Try physical separation first, then a chemical break-down.
questions.examMistake: Not distinguishing the physical test (mixture) from the chemical test (element vs compound).

questions.modelAnswer: A palisade cell has many chloroplasts and is near the top of the leaf to absorb light for photosynthesis; a root hair cell has no chloroplasts (it is underground in the dark) but has a long projection for a large surface area to absorb water and minerals.
questions.hint: One captures light, one absorbs water.
questions.examMistake: Giving features without linking them to the job.

questions.q: Explain the difference between a random error and a systematic error, and how each can be reduced.
questions.modelAnswer: A random error causes small differences each time you measure (e.g. timing with a stopwatch); it is reduced by repeating readings and taking a mean. A systematic error is a fault in the method or equipment that shifts every reading the same way (e.g. using a beaker not a measuring cylinder); repeats do not fix it — you must change the method or equipment.
questions.hint: One is fixed by repeats, the other isn't.
questions.examMistake: Saying 'repeat the experiment' fixes systematic errors — it does not.

questions.q: Identical twins have exactly the same genes but can still differ slightly. Use this to explain inherited vs environmental variation.
questions.modelAnswer: Identical twins come from one fertilised egg that split, so they have the same genes and therefore identical inherited features such as natural eye colour and blood group. Any differences between them — such as one being more muscular, having a scar, or speaking a different language — must be caused by the environment and lifestyle, not genes. This shows that some variation is inherited (genetic) and some is caused by the environment.
questions.hint: Same genes → differences must come from where?
questions.examMistake: Claiming twin differences are genetic — they can't be if the genes are identical.

questions.q: Fertiliser runs off a farm into a lake, causing algae to grow rapidly then the fish to die. Explain this sequence.
questions.modelAnswer: The fertiliser is rich in nutrients, so algae grow very fast and cover the water surface (an algal bloom). This blocks light from plants below, which die and are broken down by bacteria. The bacteria multiply and use up the dissolved oxygen in respiration, so the fish do not have enough oxygen and die. This process is called eutrophication.
questions.hint: Algae bloom → blocks light → bacteria → oxygen.
questions.examMistake: Saying the fertiliser poisons the fish directly — it's the oxygen loss that kills them.

questions.q: A roller coaster car at the top of a hill has a gravitational store. Describe the energy transfers as it goes down and speeds up.
questions.modelAnswer: As the car goes down, energy is transferred from the gravitational store to the kinetic store, so it speeds up. Some energy is also transferred to thermal (heat) and sound stores by friction and air resistance, which is 'wasted'. The total energy stays the same.
questions.hint: Where does the height energy go as it speeds up?
questions.examMistake: Forgetting that some energy is wasted as heat/sound.

questions.q: Explain why there is no sound in space.
questions.modelAnswer: Space is a vacuum, which means there are no particles. Sound needs particles to pass the vibrations from one to the next, so without any particles sound cannot travel through space.
questions.hint: What does sound need that space doesn't have?
questions.examMistake: Saying space is 'too cold' — the real reason is no particles.

mocks.title: Paper 2 — Mock 4
mocks.instructions: Short-answer + skills questions. Total 47 marks. 45 minutes.

questions.q: Using the levels of organisation, explain how a single muscle cell relates to the whole circulatory system.
questions.modelAnswer: Many similar muscle cells make up muscle tissue. Muscle tissue, together with other tissues, forms the heart, which is an organ. The heart works with blood vessels (other organs/tissues) to form the circulatory organ system, which is part of the whole organism. So a muscle cell is the smallest building block that ultimately builds the circulatory system.
questions.hint: Build up cell → tissue → organ → system.
questions.examMistake: Skipping levels or calling the heart a tissue.

questions.q: Explain the difference between a random error and a systematic error, and how each can be reduced.
questions.modelAnswer: A random error causes small differences each time you measure (e.g. timing with a stopwatch); it is reduced by repeating readings and taking a mean. A systematic error is a fault in the method or equipment that shifts every reading the same way (e.g. using a beaker not a measuring cylinder); repeats do not fix it — you must change the method or equipment.
questions.hint: One is fixed by repeats, the other isn't.
questions.examMistake: Saying 'repeat the experiment' fixes systematic errors — it does not.

questions.modelAnswer: Distillation works because heating the sea water evaporates pure water vapour, leaving the dissolved salts behind; the vapour is condensed back to pure liquid water. It is expensive because a lot of energy (heat) is needed to boil large volumes of water, and energy/fuel costs money.
questions.hint: What stays behind, and why is the energy a problem?
questions.examMistake: Not explaining why energy makes it costly.

questions.q: Explain why taking repeat readings improves the mean but cannot remove a systematic error, using an example.
questions.modelAnswer: Repeats let you average out random errors that scatter above and below the true value, so the mean is closer to the true value. A systematic error shifts every reading by the same amount in the same direction (e.g. a stopwatch always started 0.5 s late, or a balance with a zero error), so the average is still wrong by that amount. Repeating does not detect or remove it — you must fix the method or equipment.
questions.hint: Random errors cancel out; a constant shift does not.
questions.examMistake: Claiming averaging fixes all errors.

questions.q: Explain why diffusion happens faster in a gas than in a liquid.
questions.modelAnswer: Diffusion happens because particles move randomly and spread out. In a gas the particles have much more energy, move much faster and have large spaces to move into, so they spread out and mix more quickly than in a liquid, where particles move more slowly and are packed closely together.
questions.hint: Compare particle speed and spacing in gas vs liquid.
questions.examMistake: Forgetting to compare both speed and spacing.

questions.q: Describe two adaptations of a polar bear and link each to survival in the Arctic.
questions.modelAnswer: A polar bear has thick fur and a layer of fat (blubber) which insulate it and reduce heat loss in the freezing cold. It also has white fur for camouflage against the snow, so it can creep up on prey such as seals without being seen. (Large feet also spread its weight on ice and snow.)
questions.hint: Pick two features and say what each is FOR.
questions.examMistake: Listing features without explaining how they help it survive.

questions.q: Give three ways the properties of metals differ from non-metals.
questions.modelAnswer: Metals are usually shiny while non-metals are often dull. Metals are good conductors of heat and electricity, whereas non-metals are usually poor conductors (insulators). Metals are malleable and can be bent or hammered into shape, while solid non-metals are brittle and snap. (Metals also generally have higher melting points and most are solid at room temperature.)
questions.hint: Appearance, conductivity, and what happens when you bend them.
questions.examMistake: Giving only one property or vague answers like 'metals are hard'.

questions.q: Why should you heat a solution gently and slowly when crystallising, rather than boiling it dry?
questions.modelAnswer: Heating gently and stopping before it is fully dry, then letting it cool slowly, lets large, regular crystals form. Boiling it dry too fast forms only a tiny powder and can spit hot solid out, which is dangerous and gives poor crystals.
questions.hint: What forms if you cool slowly vs boil dry?
questions.examMistake: Thinking faster heating gives better crystals.

questions.q: Sound travels at about 340 m/s and light at 300,000,000 m/s. Explain how you could estimate how far away a thunderstorm is, and why the method works.
questions.modelAnswer: You count the seconds between seeing the lightning flash and hearing the thunder. Because light travels almost instantly over these distances, you effectively see the lightning at the moment it happens. The sound then takes time to reach you at about 340 m/s, so the gap in seconds tells you the travel time of the sound. Multiplying the time by 340 (or roughly, every 3 seconds ≈ 1 km) gives the distance to the storm.
questions.hint: The delay is essentially the sound's travel time.
questions.examMistake: Forgetting that the delay exists because sound is the slow one.

questions.q: A 3 V cell lights two identical bulbs. Compare the brightness and the voltage across each bulb when they are connected (a) in series and (b) in parallel.
questions.modelAnswer: In series, the 3 V is shared, so each bulb has about 1.5 V across it and both are dim. In parallel, each branch gets the full 3 V across each bulb, so each bulb is bright (as bright as a single bulb). Parallel bulbs are brighter because they each receive the full voltage.
questions.hint: How is the 3 V divided in each arrangement?
questions.examMistake: Saying parallel bulbs share the voltage — they each get the full voltage.

questions.q: Explain why scientists use a stain such as iodine when looking at cells under the microscope.
questions.modelAnswer: Cells are mostly colourless and transparent, so their parts are hard to see. A stain such as iodine is absorbed by certain structures (e.g. it stains starch and makes the nucleus and other parts darker), increasing the contrast so the cell parts can be seen clearly.
questions.hint: Cells are see-through — what does colour add?
questions.examMistake: Saying the stain 'feeds' the cell — it only adds contrast.

questions.q: Explain the difference between current and voltage.
questions.modelAnswer: Current is the flow of electric charge around the circuit, measured in amps with an ammeter in series. Voltage (potential difference) is the energy or 'push' supplied by the cell to drive that current, measured in volts with a voltmeter in parallel. Voltage causes the current to flow.
questions.hint: One is the flow, one is the push.
questions.examMistake: Treating volts and amps as the same thing.

questions.q: Explain why offspring resemble their parents but are not identical to them (in sexual reproduction).
questions.modelAnswer: Offspring inherit genes from both parents, carried on chromosomes in the nucleus, so they show features from each parent and therefore resemble them. However, because they get a mixture of genes — half from each parent in a unique combination — they are not identical to either parent or to their siblings.
questions.hint: Genes come from two parents in a new combination.
questions.examMistake: Forgetting that genes come from BOTH parents.
